messages.content:
This section corresponds to **Kapitel 7-1** (Time Expressions: *vor, seit, schon*) and **Kapitel 7-2** (*Ein*-word Adjective Endings) from the German 201 curriculum.

### **Kapitel 7-1: Time Expressions**
This module covers how to express duration and point-in-time references using the dative case.

*   ***vor* (Ago):** Used with the **dative case** to denote a point in time in the past (e.g., *vor einem Monat* – a month ago).
*   ***seit* (Since/For):** Used with the **dative case**. In German, it requires the **present tense** if the action is still ongoing (e.g., *Wir wohnen seit vier Monaten in Alabama* – We have been living in Alabama for four months).
*   ***schon* (Already/Emphasizing duration):** Used with the **accusative case** to indicate that an action has been and remains ongoing. Like *seit*, it requires the **present tense**.

### **Kapitel 7-2: *Ein*-word Adjective Endings**
This module focuses on applying adjective endings when using *ein*-words (indefinite articles like *ein, mein, kein, unser*).

*   **The *Ein*-word vs. *Der*-word Rule:** Most endings are identical to *der*-word endings. The **only three exceptions** occur when the *ein*-word cannot clearly express the gender/case of the noun, requiring the ending to fulfill that grammatical function:
    *   **Nominative Masculine:** *Ein alt**er** Mann* (adds *-er*).
    *   **Nominative Neuter:** *Ein alt**es** Buch* (adds *-es*).
    *   **Accusative Neuter:** *Ein alt**es** Buch* (adds *-es*).
*   **Plural Exception:** If a plural noun is used without a definite or indefinite article, it receives an **-e** ending instead of -en (e.g., *braune Schuhe*).
*   **Comparative/Superlative:** When these are placed directly in front of a noun, they function as adjectives and **must** take the appropriate adjective ending (e.g., *Ich habe das best**e** Restaurant gefunden*).

***

**Source References:**
*   *Time Expressions* (p. 7–9)
*   *Ein-word Adjective Endings* (p. 14, 17–18, 26, 34)

Lesson 3: German 201: Intermediate I – Subordinate clauses, Cases, Travel - Presentation5 slides
German 201: Intermediate I
  • Review of core grammatical concepts
  • Focus on advanced structures and travel
  • Mastering subordinate clauses and cases

This document serves as the syllabus and course textbook for *German 201* authored by Dr. Rebecca Linam at the University of North Alabama [S.3]. The material is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License [S.4].

### Content Overview
The textbook is organized into three primary chapters, each covering specific grammatical and linguistic topics [S.5, S.7]:

1. Kapitel 7: Focuses on time expressions (*vor, seit, schon*), *ein*-word adjective endings, modal verbs in the simple past, reflexive verbs (accusative), and the *Plusquamperfekt* tense.
2. Kapitel 8: Covers the simple past (*Präteritum*), the distinctions between *als, wenn,* and *wann*, infinitive phrases (with *zu, um…zu, ohne…zu*), and verbs such as *lassen, verlassen,* and *gehen*.
3. Kapitel 9: Addresses reflexive verbs (dative), verbs with prepositional complements, *da-* and *wo-* compounds, participles, and adjectival nouns.

### Pedagogical Features
The resource is designed to provide a comprehensive learning experience through several integrated tools:
* Interactive Learning: Includes links to external sites like *germanzone.org* and Deutsche Welle’s *Nicos Weg* for interactive, automatically graded exercises [S.8].
* Supplementary Materials: Each chapter includes reading selections (including *Till Eulenspiegel*), short articles, dialogues, comic strips, and proficiency interview practice [S.7, S.8].
* Multimedia Support: The text incorporates links to videos reteaching grammar concepts, pronunciation guides, and German music to provide cultural context [S.7, S.8].
* Design Philosophy: The material emphasizes gradual vocabulary acquisition, comparisons between English and German, and flexibility—allowing instructors to rearrange sections to suit their specific curriculum needs [S.8].

This section provides a structured review of the German conversational past tense ($Perfekt$) covered in GR 102.

**Forming the Conversational Past**
The $Perfekt$ tense consists of a helping verb ($haben$ or $sein$) and a past participle at the end of the sentence (p. 9).

**Helping Verb Selection**
*   Use $haben$ for verbs that do not involve movement from point A to B (p. 9).
*   Use $sein$ for verbs involving movement (A to B), as well as for $bleiben$ (to stay) and $werden$ (to become) (p. 9).

**The Past Participle**
*   Regular verbs end in $-t$ (e.g., $gespielt$), while irregular verbs typically end in $-en$ (e.g., $getrunken$) (p. 9-10).
*   The "90% Rule" suggests that German verbs often follow the same regularity pattern as their English counterparts (p. 10).
*   Verb patterns: $ei$ often switches to $ie$ in irregular verbs (p. 10).

**Special Conjugation Rules**
*   $-ieren$ verbs: Always regular (end in $-t$) and never take the $ge-$ prefix (p. 10).
*   Inseparable prefix verbs: Do not take the $ge-$ prefix (p. 10).
*   Separable prefix verbs: The $ge-$ prefix is placed between the prefix and the verb root (p. 10).
*   Mixed verbs: A small group of verbs ($bringen$, $kennen$, $nennen$, $rennen$, $wissen$) are technically irregular but end in $-t$. It is recommended to memorize these (p. 10-11).

**Additional Resources**
The text concludes by linking to interactive exercises, video tutorials, and listening comprehension practice to help refresh knowledge of both the conversational past and the simple past forms of $haben$ and $sein$ (p. 11-13).

This summary covers the time expressions and adjective endings presented in the provided text (pages [S.15]–[S.36]).

### Time Expressions: vor, seit, schon

*   **Vor**: Used to express "ago." It always takes the **dative case** (e.g., *vor zehn Minuten*) [S.15].
*   **Seit**: Used to express how long an action has been ongoing. In German, this requires the **present tense** (unlike English, which uses the past tense for similar contexts). It is followed by the **dative case** (e.g., *seit vier Monaten*) [S.16, S.17].
*   **Schon**: Implies the action is still continuing, similar to *seit*. However, *schon* is followed by the **accusative case** [S.18].

### Adjective Endings

Adjective endings depend on the article used before the noun. 

**Der-Word Endings (Definite articles or similar)**
These endings are determined by the case (Nominative, Accusative, Dative, Gentive) and the gender of the noun.
*   All plurals in this category take –en.
*   All dative and genitive forms take –en [S.22].

**Ein-Word Endings (Indefinite articles or similar)**
Most endings are identical to *der*-word endings, with three specific exceptions where *ein*-words must explicitly show the gender:
1. Nominative Masculine: Add –er (e.g., *ein alter Mann*) [S.25].
2. Nominative Neuter: Add –es (e.g., *ein altes Buch*) [S.26].
3. Accusative Neuter: Add –es (e.g., *ein altes Buch*) [S.26].

**Plural Exception**
If a plural noun does not have a definite or indefinite article in front of it, it adds an –e ending instead of the –en seen in the chart [S.27]. 

**Comparative and Superlative**
When adjectives in the comparative or superlative form are placed directly before a noun, they must also receive the appropriate adjective ending (e.g., *Du hast einen kleineren Koffer*). When used without a noun, they do not take these endings [S.34, S.35].

### Summary of Reflexive Verbs (Accusative)

Reflexive verbs occur when the subject of a sentence performs an action upon itself, meaning the subject and the direct object are the same entity (p. 49, 50).

**Key Components**
* Reflexive Marker: German uses specific reflexive pronouns (such as *sich*) to indicate that a verb is reflexive (p. 50).
* Infinitive Form: Many reflexive verbs are identified in the dictionary with *sich* attached to the infinitive (p. 50).
* Conjugation Rule: When conjugating these verbs, the reflexive pronoun (*sich*) is placed immediately after the conjugated verb (p. 51).

**Essential Notes**
* Cross-Language Differences: A verb that is reflexive in German may not necessarily be reflexive in English. It is necessary to learn which German verbs require the reflexive marker as they do not carry over one-to-one (p. 51).
* Non-Reflexive vs. Reflexive: Contrast the action between two different objects (e.g., "washing the dog") versus the subject acting upon itself (e.g., "washing herself") to determine if a verb is reflexive (p. 49, 50).

This section provides an overview of German reflexive verbs using the accusative case (p. 54-57). Here is a summary of the key concepts:

Reflexive Pronouns
German reflexive pronouns change based on the grammatical subject. While the pronoun "sich" remains consistent for third-person subjects, other pronouns change to match the subject, functioning similarly to standard accusative pronouns (p. 54).

Definition and Usage
Reflexive verbs are used when the subject and the object of the verb are the same person or thing (p. 56). For example, while "Der Mann liebt die Frau" describes two different people, "Der Mann liebt sich" indicates that the man loves himself (p. 56).

Creating Reflexive Verbs
Many standard verbs can be made reflexive simply by adding the appropriate reflexive pronoun. When transitioning a sentence to a reflexive structure, the action is turned back upon the subject of the sentence (p. 56-57).

Key Practical Application
Mastery of these verbs involves identifying when an action is performed on oneself rather than on another person or object. This is essential for talking about daily routines, such as dressing (*sich anziehen*), washing (*sich waschen*), or rushing (*sich beeilen*), as well as describing emotional states or physical accidents (p. 55, 57).

This section covers German language exercises and grammar lessons, focusing on vocabulary, conversational interactions, and tense structures.

**Communication and Daily Routine (p. 58-59)**
Students are tasked with practicing conversational German through roleplay, including explaining missed classes to a professor and scheduling doctor’s appointments (p. 58). There is a significant emphasis on using reflexive verbs to describe daily routines ($Tagesablauf$) and household chores ($Hausarbeit$). Students are also directed to watch episodes 13 and 14 of *Nicos Weg* to practice household vocabulary, the expression *seit* + dative, and dative pronouns (p. 59).

**Grammar: Plusquamperfekt (p. 60)**
The text introduces the *Plusquamperfekt* (Past Perfect) tense. It notes that while this tense functions similarly to the conversational past (*Perfekt*) learned in Chapter 5, the two differ solely in the choice of the helping verb. To form the *Plusquamperfekt*, speakers use the simple past form (Präteritum) of the helping verb ($haben$ or $sein$) paired with the past participle.

According to the provided text, the sections covering pages 109–152 (Chapter 9) focus on advanced grammatical structures and syntax. Below is a concise summary of these key points:

Reflexive Verbs with Dative (Kapitel 9-1, p. 109)
Unlike accusative reflexive verbs, dative reflexive verbs involve a reflexive pronoun in the dative case. This typically occurs when the body part or object involved in the action is explicitly mentioned or when the subject performs an action for themselves (e.g., Ich wasche mir die Hände).

Verbs with Prepositional Complements (Kapitel 9-2, p. 123)
Certain German verbs require specific prepositions to complete their meaning. These fixed combinations must be learned alongside the verb, as the choice of preposition is often idiomatic rather than literal.

Da- and Wo-compounds (Kapitel 9-3, p. 137)
These compounds are used to refer to inanimate objects or abstract concepts when followed by a preposition. 
Da-compounds are formed by prefixing "da-" (or "dar-" before a vowel) to a preposition (e.g., dafür, daran, darunter). These typically function in statements.
Wo-compounds are formed by prefixing "wo-" (or "wor-" before a vowel) to a preposition (e.g., wofür, woran, worunter) and are used primarily in questions or relative clauses.

Present and Past Participles (Kapitel 9-4, p. 153)
This section addresses the use of participles as adjectives. Present participles are formed by adding "-d" to the infinitive (equivalent to the English "-ing" form), while past participles (often used in the perfect tense) function as adjectives to describe a state or completed action. Both forms are subject to adjective ending rules.

Based on the provided text, the sections covering pages 153 through 172 focus on advanced grammatical structures in German. Here is a summary of the key concepts:

**Present and Past Participles (9-4, pp. 153-159)**
These can be used as adjectives to modify nouns. Present participles are formed by adding -d to the infinitive (e.g., singend). They indicate an ongoing action. Past participles, commonly used for completed states or passive descriptions, follow standard adjective ending rules once formed (e.g., das geliebte Kind).

**Adjectival Nouns (9-5, pp. 162-172)**
German allows adjectives to function as nouns, usually referring to people. These follow the appropriate adjective declension based on the article or context. For example, "der Kranke" (the sick person) or "ein Bekannter" (an acquaintance). When these words represent abstract concepts, they are neuter and typically follow the weak declension rules (e.g., "das Schöne").

**Adjective Endings Review**
These sections reinforce the necessity of applying correct adjective endings—determined by gender, case, and the type of preceding determiner (definite, indefinite, or zero article)—when using participles or adjectival nouns. Proper application is essential for maintaining grammatical agreement within a sentence.

The provided document encompasses the structural overview and introductory grammar review for a German 201 course. 

The course curriculum is divided into three main chapters, covering advanced grammatical structures such as reflexive verbs (dative and accusative), simple past (Präteritum) versus conversational past (Perfekt), infinitive phrases, and complex prepositional usage. Supplemental materials include links to interactive exercises, music culture, and video series like "Nicos Weg".

A foundational review from the previous course (GR 102) focuses on the "Perfekt" tense, establishing the following key rules for forming the conversational past:

1. Auxiliary Verbs: Haben is used for most verbs, while sein is reserved for verbs of motion, state changes, or the specific verbs bleiben and werden (p. 1).
2. Regular vs. Irregular Participles: Regular verbs end in -t and irregular verbs typically end in -en. A useful heuristic is that if a verb is irregular in English, it has a 90% chance of being irregular in German (p. 2).
3. Prefixes: Verbs ending in -ieren and inseparable prefix verbs do not take a "ge-" prefix. Conversely, separable prefix verbs place the "ge-" between the prefix and the verb root (p. 2).
4. Mixed Verbs: A small subset of verbs is irregular but ends in -t; these must be memorized individually (e.g., bringen—gebracht) (p. 2-3).

The text begins its first new unit on time expressions in Chapter 7, noting that the preposition "vor" takes the dative case when used to indicate time "ago" (p. 7). The final section (pp. 179-183) provides glossary lists for Chapters 7, 8, and 9 to support vocabulary acquisition throughout the semester.

The provided document does not contain pages 184 through 192. According to the document's Table of Contents, the content ends at page 184 ("Reading Selections"), but the actual text terminates at page 7. Therefore, I cannot summarize the requested range.
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: If you are describing an action that began in the past and is still continuing in the present, what tense should you use in your German sentence?
options: The Conversational Past (Perfekt)
The Simple Past (Präteritum)
The Present Tense
The Plusquamperfekt
questions.explanation: In German, when using 'seit' to indicate an action that is still ongoing, the present tense is used, whereas English often uses the present perfect continuous.

questions.question: How do you form the past participle of a verb ending in '-ieren'?
options: Add a 'ge-' prefix and a '-t' suffix.
Add only a '-t' suffix without a 'ge-' prefix.
Add only an '-en' suffix without a 'ge-' prefix.
Add a 'ge-' prefix and an '-en' suffix.
questions.explanation: Verbs ending in -ieren are always regular (ending in -t), but they specifically do not take the 'ge-' prefix in the past participle.

questions.question: What is the primary grammatical reason that the adjective ending for 'ein' (masculine, nominative) is '-er' while 'der' (masculine, nominative) is '-e'?
options: Because 'ein' is always treated as an accusative trigger.
To distinguish the gender of the noun when the article ('ein') is gender-neutral.
To indicate that the verb must be in the past tense.
To avoid repeating the same sound twice in the sentence.
questions.explanation: Because 'ein' can be either masculine or neuter, the adjective ending adds the suffix of the article (-er for masculine/der, -es for neuter/das) to clarify the gender of the noun.

questions.question: When forming the past participle of a separable prefix verb, where is the 'ge-' placed?
options: Directly after the verb stem, before the suffix.
It is omitted entirely.
Between the prefix and the verb stem.
At the very beginning of the word, before the prefix.
questions.explanation: For separable verbs, the 'ge-' is inserted between the separable prefix and the verb stem (e.g., 'angekommen').

questions.question: When using 'vor' in a time expression, what case must follow it?
options: Nominative
Genitive
Accusative
Dative
questions.explanation: When 'vor' is used to denote time ('ago'), it is a dative preposition and must be followed by a noun in the dative case.

cards.front: How do you distinguish German past tense verbs that use 'haben' versus 'sein' as a helping verb?
cards.back: Use 'haben' for verbs that do not involve travel from point A to B. Use 'sein' for verbs involving movement from point A to B, as well as 'bleiben' (to stay/remain) and 'werden' (to become).

cards.front: What is the '90% rule' for determining if a German past participle is regular or irregular?
cards.back: If a verb is regular in English (e.g., played), there is a 90% chance it is regular in German (ending in -t). If it is irregular in English (e.g., drunk), there is a 90% chance it is irregular in German (ending in -en).

cards.front: What is the rule for forming the past participle of verbs ending in -ieren?
cards.back: Verbs ending in -ieren are always regular (ending in -t) and do not take the 'ge-' prefix at the beginning.

cards.front: When using 'vor' as a time expression, what case must follow it?
cards.back: It is always followed by the dative case.

cards.front: Why is the present tense used with 'seit' in German, even when the English equivalent uses the past tense?
cards.back: Because the action is still ongoing in the present; you have not yet stopped doing the activity.

cards.front: What is the primary grammatical difference between using 'seit' and 'schon' regarding the noun they modify?
cards.back: 'Seit' is followed by the dative case, while 'schon' is followed by the accusative case.

cards.front: What are the three specific cases where 'ein-word' adjective endings differ from 'der-word' adjective endings?
cards.back: 1) Nominative masculine (ein + adjective ending -er), 2) Nominative neuter (ein + adjective ending -es), 3) Accusative neuter (ein + adjective ending -es).

cards.front: How do you apply adjective endings to plural nouns that have NO definite or indefinite article in front of them?
cards.back: They take an -e ending instead of the standard -en ending used with articles.

cards.front: What is the rule for forming the past participle of separable prefix verbs?
cards.back: Place 'ge-' between the prefix and the verb (e.g., anrufen -> angerufen).

cards.front: How do you form the past participle of inseparable prefix verbs?
cards.back: They do not take the 'ge-' prefix at all (e.g., besuchen -> besucht).

slides.title: German 201: Intermediate I
bullets: Focus on advanced structures and travel
slides.notes: Welcome to German 201. This course builds on foundational knowledge while introducing advanced concepts for intermediate fluency.

slides.title: Review: Conversational Past
slides.notes: We begin by refreshing our understanding of the Perfect tense, ensuring solid control over auxiliary verbs and participle formation.

slides.title: Time Expressions: Vor, Seit, Schon
bullets: Use present tense with seit and schon
slides.notes: Time expressions are crucial for narration; distinguishing between ago, since, and already requires attention to case and tense.

slides.title: Accusative Reflexive Verbs
bullets: Action reflects back on the subject
slides.notes: Reflexive verbs identify actions performed by subjects upon themselves; learning these markers is essential for accurate descriptions.

slides.title: The Plusquamperfekt
bullets: Uses simple past of auxiliary verb
slides.notes: The Plusquamperfekt allows us to describe sequences of events in the past, adding sophisticated timing to your storytelling.
